front: Dynasty: founded by the peasant rebel Liu Bang, who overthrew this dynasty's predecessor
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: gives its name to China's majority ethnic group
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: ruled roughly 206 BCE to 220 CE, conventionally divided into a Western/Former and an Eastern/Later period
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: its height was roughly contemporaneous with the Roman Empire under Augustus
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Ruler: this dynasty's founder defeated his rival Xiang Yu at the Battle of Gaixia, ending years of civil war
work: Founding: Liu Bang and the Chu-Han Contention

front: Event: before their final war, Xiang Yu tried to assassinate this dynasty's founder at a banquet
work: Founding: Liu Bang and the Chu-Han Contention

front: Event: the establishment of the rival Eighteen Kingdoms began this dynasty's contention with Xiang Yu's state of Western Chu
work: Founding: Liu Bang and the Chu-Han Contention

front: Ruler: legend holds that while drunk, this dynasty's founder killed the 'White Emperor' after cutting off the head of a white snake
work: Founding: Liu Bang and the Chu-Han Contention

front: Ruler: Huai II made this dynasty's founder the King of Guanzhong after he captured the city of Xianyang
work: Founding: Liu Bang and the Chu-Han Contention

front: Ruler: this dynasty's first empress became the first Empress of China; she had the general Peng Yue killed and was accused of murdering three princes of Zhao
work: Empress Lu and the Early Succession Crisis

front: Ruler: this dynasty's Empress Dowager Lu turned Concubine Qi into a 'human swine' by cutting off her limbs and gouging out her eyes
work: Empress Lu and the Early Succession Crisis

front: Ruler: this dynasty's founder died from an injury sustained while campaigning against the rebel Ying Bu, declaring even Bian Que could not save him
work: Empress Lu and the Early Succession Crisis

front: Figure: this scholar wrote the Records of the Grand Historian, chronicling much of this dynasty's early history
work: Emperor Wu (Wudi) and Sima Qian

front: Figure: this historian was castrated rather than executed for defending general Li Ling, who had defected to the Xiongnu
work: Emperor Wu (Wudi) and Sima Qian

front: Institution: an emperor established this Confucian institution after banning the Hundred Schools of Thought
work: Emperor Wu (Wudi) and Sima Qian

front: Institution: instituted state monopolies on salt and iron production; official Sang Hongyang extended this with an 'equable marketing system' for grain
work: Salt and Iron Monopolies

front: Institution: the salt and iron monopolies helped fund this dynasty's southward conquest of the Yue
work: Salt and Iron Monopolies

front: Institution: these monopolies were never revived after Emperor Zhang's death, per the histories of Fan Ye and Ban Gu
work: Salt and Iron Monopolies

front: Event: after a defeat at the Battle of Baideng, this dynasty adopted a policy of marrying tribute princesses to Xiongnu chanyu, including Wang Zhaojun's marriage to Huhanye
work: Xiongnu Wars and the Heqin Marriage-Alliance Policy

front: Figure: this general led an expedition into the Western Regions that captured the cities of Kara Shahr and Kucha
work: Xiongnu Wars and the Heqin Marriage-Alliance Policy

front: Figure: this dynasty's diplomat Lady Feng Liao averted a conflict, while Princess Liu Xijun wrote a melancholic poem calling her new home 'the edge of the world'
work: Xiongnu Wars and the Heqin Marriage-Alliance Policy

front: Work: this text by the historian Ban Zhao details four ideal, submissive feminine virtues
back: Lessons for Women (Ban Zhao, Han Dynasty)
work: Ban Zhao and Women's Education

front: Figure: this historian finished her brother Ban Gu's history after his imprisonment and death
work: Ban Zhao and Women's Education

front: Event: a law during this dynasty quintupled the poll tax for families with unmarried daughters between the ages of 15 and 30
work: Ban Zhao and Women's Education

front: Event: under Emperor Wu, this dynasty conquered the Nanyue and Dian kingdoms as part of its subjugation of the 'Hundred Yue,' including the Trieu dynasty
work: Southward Expansion: Nanyue and the Trung Sisters' Rebellion

front: Event: this pair of sisters led a rebellion against Han rule in Vietnam, remembered riding into battle on war elephants
work: Southward Expansion: Nanyue and the Trung Sisters' Rebellion

front: Event: this dynasty campaigned against Minyue in retaliation for an invasion of the Eastern Ou, and dominated the Giao Chi people
work: Southward Expansion: Nanyue and the Trung Sisters' Rebellion

front: Event: merchants of this dynasty trading with 'southwest barbarians' reported a Sendhuk community, thought to be a lost Burmese state or an Indus Valley settlement
work: Southward Expansion: Nanyue and the Trung Sisters' Rebellion

front: Ruler: this Confucian scholar-official deposed the child Emperor Ping and founded his own Xin Dynasty, interrupting this dynasty's rule
work: Wang Mang's Xin Dynasty Interregnum

front: Event: peasants painted their eyebrows red to rebel against Wang Mang's usurpation, eventually restoring this dynasty
work: Wang Mang's Xin Dynasty Interregnum

front: Institution: Wang Mang attempted to revive this ancient land-distribution system during his usurpation of this dynasty
work: Wang Mang's Xin Dynasty Interregnum

front: Institution: this dynasty created the zhou, a higher administrative subdivision above roughly 160 commanderies
work: Administration and Science

front: Figure: this polymath invented a hydraulic armillary sphere and a seismometer while working at this dynasty's second capital, Luoyang
work: Administration and Science

front: Event: this dynasty collected tribute in kind, paying officials in dan of grain, with the highest salary going to the Three Ducal Ministers
work: Administration and Science

front: Event: in 1968, explosives unearthed this dynasty's mausoleum for Liu Sheng of Zhongshan, where molten metal had sealed off Dou Wan's jade burial suit
work: Administration and Science

front: Event: this Daoist movement, preaching the 'Way of Supreme Peace,' was led by the Zhang brothers and greatly weakened this dynasty
work: Decline and Fall: the Yellow Turban Rebellion

front: Event: the Daoist Celestial Masters sect led this rebellion, in which peasants donated grain to join their state
back: Five Pecks of Rice Rebellion (Han Dynasty)
work: Decline and Fall: the Yellow Turban Rebellion

front: Ruler: a succession struggle between Empress He, her brother He Jin, and court eunuchs let this warlord seize power over this dynasty
work: Decline and Fall: the Yellow Turban Rebellion

front: Event: this dynasty formally ended when its Emperor Xian abdicated to Cao Pi, son of the warlord Cao Cao
work: Decline and Fall: the Yellow Turban Rebellion

front: Figure: this dynasty's general deposed Prince He after twenty-eight days, prompting an emperor to call him a 'thorn in my back'
work: Decline and Fall: the Yellow Turban Rebellion

front: Event: intrigue between scholar-officials and student 'partisans' (dangren) prompted a civil-liberties crackdown in this dynasty, later lifted by Emperor Ling
work: Decline and Fall: the Yellow Turban Rebellion
